frameworks.name: dynamic logistic regression (time-varying psychophysical GLM)
frameworks.one_line: Trial-by-trial weights on stimulus and task-history covariates that drift smoothly across training.

frameworks.name: signal-detection / psychophysical observer
frameworks.one_line: Static psychometric weights are extended to time-varying weights tracking learning.

frameworks.name: Laplace approximation / empirical Bayes
frameworks.one_line: Decoupled Laplace approximation maximizes marginal likelihood over evolution-rate hyperparameters.

core_question: How can we efficiently infer trial-to-trial changes in an animal's psychophysical policy as it learns a sensory discrimination task?
key_assumptions: Behavior is captured by a logistic mapping from stimulus and task-history covariates to choice.
Each weight evolves smoothly over trials with its own evolution rate.
Marginal-likelihood hyperparameter optimization is tractable via decoupled Laplace approximation.
manipulations: Time horizon: trial-to-trial vs day-to-day weight dynamics.
Covariate set: sensory stimulus plus task-irrelevant history (stimulus, choice, answer).
Species: rats vs human subjects on a delayed sensory discrimination task.
key_findings: Method scales to ~500K parameters (10 weights × 50K trials) in minutes on a desktop.
Recovers day-to-day and trial-to-trial fluctuations in rat psychophysical weights during training.
Tracks evolving choice bias and task-history dependencies underlying performance changes.
Lapses on easy trials are explained by sub-optimal weighting of known task covariates rather than random inattention.
interpretations: Time-varying GLMs give finer insight into learning than fixed psychometric fits or coarse accuracy summaries.
Apparent lapse rates partly reflect mis-weighted covariates, not irreducible noise.
